scope.title: 5T Scope Definition
scope.description: Please fill in the 5T scope information for the DFMEA analysis.

toolGuide.Interface: You selected [{{tool}}]. Consider creating Interface nodes for components to record physical/signal/energy interfaces.
toolGuide.DesignParameter: You selected [{{tool}}]. Consider creating DesignParameter nodes to define ideal/non-ideal responses and control factors.

scope.toolGuideNeedStructure: Please create a system/component in the structure tree first, then add interface/parameter nodes.

structure.title: Structure Analysis
structure.description: Build the hierarchy of System → Subsystem → Component → Design Parameter, or add interface nodes to connect different branches.
structure.empty: Click the button above to add a system node

function.title: Function Analysis
function.description: Define function, technical requirement, and specification parameters for each component.

failure.title: Failure Analysis
failure.description: Define failure mode, effect, and cause for each function. The system will automatically recommend failure modes based on the function description.

risk.title: Risk Analysis
risk.description: Evaluate Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) for each failure chain.
risk.empty: No failure chains yet — create them in the Failure Analysis step first

optimization.title: Optimization Measures
optimization.noOptimizationHint: All failure modes have AP other than H; current risk is acceptable.
optimization.mustOptimize: The following {{count}} items have AP=H and require optimization measures:

confirm.title: Confirm Creation
confirm.description: After confirmation, a DFMEA document will be created and the editor will open. You can continue to refine details in the editor.

page.title: DFMEA Generation Wizard
page.completionWarning: The following steps are incomplete and cannot be submitted:
page.step3Incomplete: Function Analysis — some components have no functions defined
page.step4Incomplete: Failure Analysis — some functions have no failure modes defined
page.step5Incomplete: Risk Analysis — some failure chains have missing S/O/D ratings
page.step5IncompleteMissingCause: Risk Analysis — some failure chains have no failure cause defined (add a cause before rating)
page.confirmEmptyDraft: The current draft is empty and will be deleted upon confirmation.
page.finishFailed: Failed to finish. Please try again.
page.conflictContent: This FMEA was modified by another session. Reload is required before editing. Your unsaved local changes will be discarded.
page.structureGap: You selected [{{tool}}] but no {{nodeType}} node exists. Consider adding one to reflect that analysis method.

step0.title: Step 1: 5T Scope Definition
step0.purpose: Define the boundaries, team, and focus of the DFMEA analysis to ensure focused follow-up steps.
step0.points: The 5T are inTent, Timing, Team, Task, Tool; this step frames the scope of analysis accordingly.

fields.name: Team
fields.desc: Cross-functional core team: manager (project manager, decides risk acceptability), design/process leads (technical responsibility), facilitator (coordinates the 7 steps), design/system/component engineers, test engineer, quality/reliability engineer; extended team: technical experts, manufacturing/maintenance/functional-safety engineers, purchasing, suppliers, customer representative.

fields.name: Timing
fields.desc: DFMEA start and end dates. Start once the design concept is well understood; complete the analysis before design freeze; finish improvement actions before tooling samples. Run in parallel with the process FMEA with open information flow.

fields.name: Tool
fields.desc: Analysis method/tools used, e.g. structure tree, block/boundary diagram, interface analysis table, parameter diagram (P-diagram), function tree/net; if no baseline exists, develop a new base DFMEA.

fields.name: Task
fields.desc: Scope and object of this analysis. Clarify: what will the customer buy? Any new requirements? Do we hold design authority? At which level (system/subsystem/component) to analyze? Who owns interface design? Is it a new design, a new application, or an engineering change?

fields.name: Trend
fields.desc: Trends of field failures, customer complaints, warranty data, and lessons learned from past similar products, used as the baseline reference and input for this analysis.

step0.example: Team: BMS Design Group, Process Engineering; Timing: 2026 Q1–Q3; Tool: structure tree + boundary diagram; Task: DC-DC Converter DFMEA; Trend: complaint statistics from 3 past similar products

step1.title: Step 2: Structure Analysis
step1.purpose: Identify and decompose the design into systems, subsystems, components, and parts for technical risk analysis.
step1.points: Organize as higher-level element — focus element — lower-level element or characteristic; any element missing here will also be missing in function analysis.

fields.name: Structure tree levels
fields.desc: Decompose the design into system → subsystem → component/part, organized as higher-level — focus element — lower-level or characteristic type, for technical risk analysis.

fields.name: Hierarchy depth
fields.desc: Generally no more than 4 levels; complex structures can be split into sub-structures (work packages). Only parts the team designs or controls go inside the boundary; parts outside can be marked.

fields.name: Interface node
fields.desc: Represents cross-branch interaction. Five interface types: physical connection, material exchange, energy transfer, data transfer, human-machine; provides main/interface function input for function analysis.

fields.name: Design Parameter
fields.desc: Lower-level characteristic types, e.g. geometric dimensions, material, surface treatment — quantifiable attributes.

fields.name: Naming
fields.desc: Name with technical terms; each component should be an independently analyzable physical unit.

step1.example: System: BMS → Subsystem: BMU / Charging Mgmt → Component: LTC6811; Interface: BMU↔Charging Mgmt (data transfer)

step2.title: Step 3: Function Analysis
step2.purpose: Ensure functions specified by requirements/specs are properly assigned to system elements, as the basis for failure analysis.
step2.points: Describe functions as action verb + measurable noun, present tense; distinguish base function, detailed requirement, and design constraint.

fields.name: Function description
fields.desc: Use action verb + measurable noun, present tense, verb base form (deliver, contain, control, transfer, acquire). E.g. transmit power, acquire cell voltage. An element can have multiple functions (main + interface/diagnostic/serviceability auxiliary functions).

fields.name: Requirement
fields.desc: What is needed to realize the function, related to functional performance. Sources: legal requirements, industry standards (ISO 9001/IATF 16949/ISO 26262), customer requirements (explicit + implicit), internal requirements, product characteristics. Distinguish base function, detailed requirement, design constraint.

fields.name: Specification
fields.desc: Quantifiable product characteristic/attribute, with tolerance. E.g. shaft diameter, surface finish, accuracy ±2mV, sample rate ≥10Hz.

step2.example: Component LTC6811 → Function: Acquire cell voltage → Requirement: Accurately acquire each cell voltage → Specification: Accuracy ±2mV, sample rate ≥10Hz

step3.title: Step 4: Failure Analysis
step3.purpose: Identify failure causes, modes, and effects and show their relationships to enable risk assessment.
step3.points: Failure chain FE←FM←FC: failure effect (on next level/end user/regulation), failure mode (opposite of function), failure cause (actionable level).

fields.name: Failure Mode (FM)
fields.desc: Ways the function may not be met, derived from the function, described in technical terms. Types: function loss, function degradation, intermittent function, partial function loss, unintended function, function out-of-range, function delay.

fields.name: Failure Effect (FE)
fields.desc: Consequence of the failure mode, describing impact on the next-level integrated product, the end user, and regulations; what the user may notice or experience, including safety. One FM can have multiple effects.

fields.name: Failure Cause (FC)
fields.desc: Why the failure mode occurs. Sources: improper functional-performance design, system interaction, time-dependent change (fatigue/wear/corrosion), external environment, user misuse, piece variation, software issues. Should be concise and complete so specific actions can target the cause.

fields.name: Chain organization
fields.desc: Organize as FE←FM←FC, i.e. higher-level effect — focus-element failure mode — lower-level failure cause; ensure the three elements are fully linked.

step3.example: Function Acquire cell voltage → Failure mode: Insufficient acquisition accuracy → Effect: Control decision deviation → Cause: Sensor aging / calibration drift

step4.title: Step 5: Risk Analysis
step4.purpose: Estimate risk by evaluating severity, occurrence, and detection, and prioritize the actions to take.
step4.points: S/O/D range 1–10 (10 highest risk contribution); S rates FE, O rates FC frequency (based on prevention control), D rates detection-control effectiveness; AP is auto-calculated by the system per S→O→D.

fields.name: Prevention Control (PC)
fields.desc: Existing/planned actions that mitigate the potential causes leading to FM; the basis for rating Occurrence O, related to performance requirements. Must be clear and complete, citing specific tests/procedures. Examples: simulation (FEA/CFD), design review, risk analysis, lessons learned, hand sample.

fields.name: Detection Control (DC)
fields.desc: Actual (planned/completed) activities that detect whether FC or FM exists before production release; the basis for rating Detection D. Must cite specific test/plan/procedure. Examples: function check, burst test, environmental test, endurance test, poka-yoke.

fields.name: Severity (S)
fields.desc: Severity of the failure effect, 1–10 (10 highest). 10=endangers vehicle/personnel safety, 9=non-compliant with regulation, 8=base function loss, 6=convenience function loss, 1=no obvious effect. Rates FE only, not O/D.

fields.name: Occurrence (O)
fields.desc: Frequency of the failure cause, 1–10, based on prevention-control effectiveness. 10=new tech with no experience and no prevention control, 5=mature tech with detail changes, 1=prevention control eliminates the cause. Reference similar-product expert opinion, warranty database, lessons learned.

fields.name: Detection (D)
fields.desc: Detectability of an occurred FC/FM, 1–10, based on the most effective detection control. 10=no test method, 5=aging test, 1=method always detects. Consider detection capability, timing, sample count, and whether the test procedure is proven.

fields.name: Action Priority (AP)
fields.desc: Auto-calculated H/M/L by the system per S→O→D logic. H=must take action, M=should, L=may. S=9–10 with AP=H/M should be reviewed by management.

step4.example: S=8 (base function loss) + O=4 (mature tech, detail change) + D=3 (failure test) → AP=H (Must optimize)

step5.title: Step 6: Optimization Measures
step5.purpose: Develop prevention and detection measures for failure chains needing further risk reduction, and assign owners.
step5.points: Optimization order: ① modify design to eliminate/reduce FE (lower S) ② reduce FC frequency (lower O) ③ improve detection (lower D); each measure needs an owner and target date.

fields.name: Prevention measure
fields.desc: Improve prevention control to lower Occurrence O. Ways: change design, improve engineering specs, design poka-yoke. Targets the failure cause FC.

fields.name: Detection measure
fields.desc: Add or change design verification to improve Detection D. Ways: design change to raise detectability, revise the test plan. Targets FC or FM.

fields.name: Reducing Severity (S)
fields.desc: Generally only achievable by design change to eliminate or reduce the failure effect FE — the highest-priority optimization direction.

fields.name: Owner and date
fields.desc: Each measure needs an owner (keeps status updated and accountable for implementation) and a realistic target completion date (per the development plan, before process validation / start of production).

fields.name: Measure status
fields.desc: Not yet defined / not yet decided / not yet executed / completed / not executed. After completion, re-evaluate O and D and update AP.

fields.name: Re-evaluation
fields.desc: On any design change, all affected elements must be re-evaluated; on a concept change, all FMEA steps must be reviewed for the affected parts.

step5.example: Prevention: redundant sensor layout → O from 4 to 2; Detection: online real-time monitoring + self-diagnosis → D from 3 to 2; Owner: Test Engineer; Target: before tooling sample

step6.title: Step 7: Confirm Creation
step6.purpose: Review data completeness across all steps, then confirm to enter the formal editor for further refinement.
step6.points: Verify structure, function, failure chains, S/O/D, and action closure one by one.

fields.name: Structure completeness
fields.desc: Does the structure tree cover all system elements, with reasonable levels and identified interfaces?

fields.name: Function coverage
fields.desc: Do all components have defined functions, requirements, and specifications?

fields.name: Failure-chain completeness
fields.desc: Does every function have a failure mode, with a complete FE/FM/FC chain?

fields.name: S/O/D ratings
fields.desc: Are S/O/D rated for every failure chain, with prevention and detection controls assigned?

fields.name: Action closure
fields.desc: Do AP=H/M items have measures, owners, target dates, and status?

step6.example: Upon confirmation, a DFMEA document will be created and the editor will open for continued revision and refinement

smartSuggestion.noGlobalPermission: (No global permission, current product line only)
smartSuggestion.tooFrequent: Too many requests, please try again later
smartSuggestion.aiUnavailable: AI suggestions unavailable, rule engine fallback used

parameterDiagram.selectNode: Please select a component node from the structure tree on the left
parameterDiagram.title: P-Diagram: {{name}}

optimizationHint.H: High priority: optimization measures must be taken to reduce Severity (S) or Occurrence (O), or improve Detection (D). Design changes or redundancy are recommended.
optimizationHint.M: Medium priority: optimization measures are recommended, focusing on improving detection means or reducing occurrence.
optimizationHint.L: Low priority: current risk is acceptable; maintain existing controls and continue monitoring.
